lead: Few things test a home like a renovation. The dust gets everywhere, the rooms fill with tools and tradespeople, and your furniture ends up shunted from corner to corner, picking up scuffs and paint splashes along the way. Over the years our Westerfield crews have helped countless Suffolk households clear the decks before the builders move in, and it transforms the whole experience. Here's how storing your belongings during a renovation protects your things, speeds up the work and keeps you sane.

sections.h: Why Move Your Belongings Out at All
sections.body:
It's tempting to think you can simply shuffle furniture around and cover it with dust sheets, and for a tiny job that might do. But for any real renovation, leaving your belongings in the house is a false economy. Building dust is astonishingly fine and pervasive; it works its way into upholstery, settles on every surface and lingers for weeks. A dust sheet slows it down but rarely stops it entirely.

Then there's the physical risk. Furniture pushed into the one finished room becomes an obstacle course for tradespeople carrying timber and tools, and it's all too easy for a wardrobe to get knocked or a sofa to catch a stray splash of paint. The more your possessions are in the way, the more likely they are to be damaged, and the slower the work goes as people work around them.

Clearing the affected rooms, or the whole house for a major project, removes both problems at a stroke. Your belongings are safe from dust and accidental damage, and the builders have clear, open space to work in. In our experience, the renovations that go most smoothly are the ones where the house is emptied of everything that isn't needed during the work, leaving the team free to crack on.

sections.h: Deciding What to Store and What to Keep
sections.body:
Not everything has to leave, and a little planning helps you decide. For a whole-house renovation where you're moving out during the work, it usually makes sense to store almost everything, since the house needs to be empty. For a project confined to one or two rooms, you can store just the contents of those areas and live around the rest, bringing furniture back as each space is finished.

Start by identifying what's at risk. Soft furnishings, upholstered items, anything wooden with a finish, electronics and anything fragile or valuable are all vulnerable to dust and knocks, so these are the priorities for storage. Heavy, hard-wearing items that can be sealed up tight matter less, though they still get in the way. The things you'll genuinely need during the work, a few essentials, perhaps the kitchen if it's untouched, can stay accessible.

Think too about the order of the work and how long each phase will take. If the kitchen is being done first and the bedrooms later, you might store in stages to match. Planning the sequence with your builder before anything moves means you store what needs storing exactly when it needs storing, and you're not paying to store rooms that aren't being touched for months. A clear plan up front saves both money and muddle.

Once you know what's going into store, prepare it properly so it comes out in good condition. Clean everything first and let it dry fully, because anything stored with a trace of damp or grime can develop mould or musty smells over a long renovation. Wipe down wood, vacuum upholstery, and give pieces time to air before they're wrapped rather than sealing in any moisture.

Dismantle furniture where it's sensible, as beds, tables and shelving store far more safely and compactly in pieces. Keep every screw and fitting in a labelled bag taped to the item, and a quick photo before you take something apart makes reassembly easy when the work is done. Wrap furniture in breathable covers rather than airtight plastic, especially solid wood, which can warp if moisture is trapped against it, and pad the corners that take the knocks.

Box up smaller items room by room, labelling each box clearly by room and contents, and keep an inventory of everything that's gone into store. This matters even more with a renovation than a move, because you may want particular things back partway through, and a good list lets you find them without unpacking everything. Mark any boxes you might need sooner so they can be kept accessible rather than buried at the back.

sections.h: Storing in Stages to Match the Work
sections.body:
One of the real advantages of storage during a renovation is the ability to work in phases, and it's something our crews handle all the time. Rather than moving everything out and back in one go, you can rotate belongings in and out to follow the build. As the builders move from room to room, the contents of the next area go into store while the finished rooms get their furniture back.

This staged approach keeps your home as liveable as possible throughout a long project. You're not living among a houseful of boxes for months on end, and you're only storing what genuinely needs to be out of the way at any one time. It also means each room can be properly finished and cleaned before its furniture returns, so nothing comes back into a half-done, dusty space.

Coordinating this with your builder's schedule is the key. A quick conversation about the order of works lets you plan the moves in advance, and our team can collect and return items in stages to fit that timetable. We've found that customers who plan the phasing early have a far calmer renovation, because the logistics of their belongings are sorted around the work rather than fought against it. It turns a potential headache into a smooth, predictable process.

sections.h: Protecting What Stays and the Rooms Around the Work
sections.body:
Not everything can be stored, and sometimes you'll be living in part of the house while another part is gutted, so protecting what stays put matters just as much as moving the rest out. Seal off the work area as best you can, because building dust travels astonishingly far through a home and finds its way into rooms you thought were safe. Closing doors, taping gaps and hanging dust sheets across openings all help contain the worst of it.

For the furniture and belongings that remain in the lived-in rooms, a good cover goes a long way. Drape sheets over sofas and shelving near the work, and keep soft furnishings and anything fragile as far from the dust as you can. Lifting rugs and protecting flooring along the route the builders walk saves a lot of cleaning later and guards against scuffs from boots and trolleys carrying materials in and out.

It's worth thinking about the practical flow of the house during the work, too. Tradespeople need a clear path to carry timber, tools and rubble, so anything left in their way is both at risk and a hindrance. Keeping that route clear, and storing the bulkier items that would otherwise block it, makes the job safer and quicker. A little planning about what stays, what's covered and what's stored keeps the whole project running smoothly.

sections.h: Bringing It All Back and How We Help
sections.body:
The best part of a renovation is finally bringing your home back to life, and that's far more enjoyable when your belongings have been safely stored rather than battered and dusty. Once the work is done and the rooms are clean, your stored furniture and boxes come back into a fresh, finished home, ready to slot into place. With a good inventory and clear labels, unpacking into the right rooms is straightforward.

Take the chance to set things up properly rather than rushing, since a newly renovated space deserves a thoughtful return. It's also a natural moment to reassess, as you may find some things you stored no longer suit the new layout, which is a useful prompt to keep, store on or pass them along. A renovation often changes how you want to use your home, and your furniture arrangement can change with it.

This whole process is far simpler with one experienced team handling both the moving and the storage. Our crews prepare, wrap, move and store your belongings, hold them securely while the work goes on, and deliver everything back to your door in stages or all at once, whatever suits, with everything fully covered while it's in our care. There's no double handling and no separate companies to coordinate. If you've got a renovation coming up and aren't sure how to manage your belongings around it, talk to us and we'll help you plan it out.

faqs: Why not just cover my furniture with dust sheets during a renovation?

Dust sheets slow the fine building dust but rarely stop it settling into upholstery and surfaces over weeks of work. Furniture left in the house also gets in the tradespeople's way and risks knocks and paint splashes. Storing the affected contents protects your belongings and lets the builders work faster in clear rooms.
Do I have to store everything, or just some rooms?

It depends on the project. For a whole-house renovation it usually makes sense to store almost everything. For work confined to one or two rooms, you can store just those contents and live around the rest, bringing furniture back as each space is finished. Plan the sequence with your builder first.
Can I get some items back partway through the work?

Yes. Storing in stages to match the build is one of the big advantages, and our crews handle this regularly. Keep a clear inventory and label boxes you might need sooner so they stay accessible. We can collect and return items in phases to fit your builder's schedule, keeping your home as liveable as possible.
How do I prepare belongings for storage during building work?

Clean and fully dry everything first to avoid mould, dismantle furniture where sensible, and wrap it in breathable covers rather than airtight plastic. Label boxes by room and contents and keep an inventory. Our team can prepare, wrap, move and store your belongings, all fully covered while in our care.
